content:
# Judges Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_272.jpg, JDG_page_273.jpg

### The Song of Deborah and Barak

1. On that day Deborah and Barak son of Abinoam sang this song:

2. "When Israel's leaders step forward,
when the people gladly volunteer —
praise the LORD!

3. Listen, you kings! Pay attention, you rulers!
I will sing to the LORD;
I will make music to the LORD, the God of Israel.

4. LORD, when you set out from Seir,
when you marched from the fields of Edom,
the earth shook, the heavens poured rain,
the clouds poured down water.

5. The mountains melted in the presence of the LORD —
even Mount Sinai — in the presence of the LORD, the God of Israel.

6. In the days of Shamgar son of Anath,
and in the days of Jael,
the roads were deserted;
travelers took to winding back roads.

7. There were no warriors in Israel —
not until I, Deborah, arose,
arose as a mother for Israel.

8. When the people chose new gods,
war came to the city gates.
Yet not a shield or spear could be found
among forty thousand warriors in Israel!

9. My heart is with you, commanders of Israel,
with those among the people who volunteer freely.
Praise the LORD!

10. Sing about it, you who ride white donkeys,
you who sit on fine saddle blankets,
and you who walk along the road!

11. Listen to the musicians singing at the watering holes!
They tell of the LORD's righteous victories,
the victories of his warriors in Israel.
Then the people of the LORD marched down to the city gates.

12. Wake up, wake up, Deborah!
Wake up, wake up, and sing a song!
Rise up, Barak!
Lead your captives away, son of Abinoam!

13. Then the survivors came down to join the nobles;
the people of the LORD came down with the warriors.

14. Some came from Ephraim, a land once rooted in Amalek;
the tribe of Benjamin followed among your ranks.
From Machir came commanders,
and from Zebulun, those who carry the officer's staff.

15. The leaders of Issachar were with Deborah;
yes, Issachar was loyal to Barak,
rushing into the valley at his heels.
But among the clans of Reuben,
there was great indecision.

16. Why did you stay among the sheep pens,
listening to the shepherds whistle for their flocks?
Yes, among the clans of Reuben,
there was great indecision.

17. Gilead remained east of the Jordan.
And why did Dan stay with the ships?
Asher sat unmoved at the seashore,
remaining in his harbors.

18. But Zebulun risked their very lives;
Naphtali, too, on the battlefield heights.

19. The kings of Canaan came and fought
at Taanach, near the waters of Megiddo,
but they carried off no silver plunder.

20. The stars fought from heaven;
from their orbits they fought against Sisera!

21. The Kishon River swept them away —
that ancient river, the river Kishon.
March on with courage, my soul!

22. Then the horses' hooves pounded the ground —
the galloping, galloping of mighty stallions!

23. 'Curse Meroz,' said the angel of the LORD.
'Curse its people bitterly,
because they refused to come help the LORD —
to help the LORD against the mighty warriors.'

24. Most blessed among women is Jael,
the wife of Heber the Kenite;
most blessed is she among tent-dwelling women.

25. Sisera asked for water,
and she gave him milk.
In a bowl fit for nobles,
she brought him yogurt.

26. With her left hand she reached for the tent peg,
with her right hand for the workman's hammer.
She struck Sisera and crushed his head,
shattering and piercing his skull.

27. At her feet he sank,
he fell, he lay still.
At her feet he sank, he fell;
where he sank, there he fell — dead.

28. Sisera's mother looked out through the window.
From behind the window lattice she cried out,
'Why is his chariot so long in coming?
Why don't we hear the clatter of his chariots?'

29. Her wisest ladies answer her;
indeed, she keeps telling herself,

30. 'They must be dividing up the captured plunder —
a woman or two for every warrior,
and beautiful dyed clothing for Sisera,
yes, colorful embroidered clothing,
two pieces of fine embroidery as plunder.'

31. LORD, may all your enemies die like Sisera!
But may those who love you rise like the sun in all its power!"

And there was peace in the land for forty years.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The Song of Deborah:** This is widely considered one of the oldest poems in the entire Bible, possibly dating to the 12th century BCE — very close to the events it describes. Its archaic Hebrew, vivid imagery, and raw emotion make it a masterpiece of ancient war poetry. It celebrates the victory of chapter 4 from a completely different angle — not narrative but lyric.

**The roll call (vv.14-18):** The poem praises the tribes who came to fight and shames those who stayed home. Ephraim, Benjamin, Machir (Manasseh), Zebulun, Issachar, and Naphtali answered the call. Reuben debated endlessly but never came. Gilead (Gad), Dan, and Asher stayed with their commercial interests. This tribal roll call reveals the fragmented, decentralized nature of pre-monarchic Israel.

**Sisera's mother (vv.28-30):** The poem's emotional climax is devastating — not a battlefield scene, but a mother looking through a window, waiting for a son who will never come home. Her ladies reassure her that he's just delayed, dividing the spoil — including captured women. The Hebrew word for these women (racham, "womb") reduces them to their reproductive organs. The poet makes us feel both the pathos of a mother's worry and the horror of what she casually assumes.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
